stakeholders.name: United States
stakeholders.role: Primary military actor and global energy consumer
stakeholders.position: Leading military operations against Iran, facing domestic economic pressure from rising energy costs

stakeholders.name: Iran
stakeholders.role: Target of attacks and regional disruptor
stakeholders.position: Retaliating through asymmetric warfare, controlling critical energy chokepoint

stakeholders.name: Israel
stakeholders.role: US military partner and regional security actor
stakeholders.position: Joint participant in strikes against Iran

stakeholders.name: Saudi Arabia
stakeholders.role: Major oil producer and regional power
stakeholders.position: Victim of Iranian attacks, critical energy infrastructure compromised

stakeholders.name: China
stakeholders.role: Major energy consumer and Iranian diplomatic partner
stakeholders.position: Attempting to mediate by pressuring Iran to reopen Strait of Hormuz

stakeholders.name: Marine Insurance Companies
stakeholders.position: Canceling war risk coverage, effectively blocking commercial shipping

stakeholders.name: European Union
stakeholders.position: Facing severe energy price spikes and supply disruptions

predictions.text: Continued escalation of military operations for several weeks based on Trump's stated timeline
predictions.basis: President Trump explicitly indicated operations may last weeks, and Iran shows no signs of backing down

predictions.text: Oil prices will reach $100+ per barrel if Strait of Hormuz remains closed
predictions.basis: 20% of global oil supply blocked, existing price trajectory from $80-85 to higher levels, historical precedent during supply disruptions

predictions.text: Global recession triggered by energy crisis and financial market instability
predictions.basis: Severe energy price spikes historically trigger recessions, major stock market declines already occurring

predictions.text: Emergency release of strategic petroleum reserves by major consuming nations
predictions.basis: Standard policy response to supply disruptions, domestic political pressure from rising gas prices

predictions.text: Chinese diplomatic intervention to broker ceasefire or Strait reopening
predictions.basis: China already pressuring Iran, has significant economic leverage and energy import needs

historicalContext: This crisis echoes the 1980s 'Tanker War' during the Iran-Iraq conflict when both sides attacked oil infrastructure and shipping, leading to Operation Earnest Will where the US Navy escorted Kuwaiti tankers. The current situation also parallels the 1973 oil embargo and 1979 Iranian Revolution energy crises that triggered global recessions. The Strait of Hormuz has been a persistent flashpoint since the 1980s, with Iran repeatedly threatening closure during periods of tension. However, this represents the first time since World War II that a major energy chokepoint has been effectively closed by military action rather than political embargo, and the first direct US-Iranian military confrontation of this scale.
implications: Global energy security paradigm shift toward diversification away from Middle Eastern chokepoints
Acceleration of renewable energy transition due to fossil fuel supply vulnerability
Potential collapse of global shipping insurance markets for conflict zones
Realignment of international alliances based on energy security needs
Severe inflationary pressure on global economy, particularly affecting developing nations
Possible breakdown of international maritime law and freedom of navigation principles
Increased militarization of energy infrastructure protection globally
Potential for wider regional war involving Gulf states and proxy forces
